title: Most Hyperelliptic Curves Over Q Have No Rational Points
speaker: Manjul Bhargava

proof_stages.label: Height-ordered hyperelliptic family and density theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines hyperelliptic curves over Q by even-degree binary forms z^2=f(x,y), orders them by coefficient height, and states that as the genus grows the proportion with rational points tends rapidly to zero, while almost all remain locally soluble and hence fail the Hasse principle.

proof_stages.label: Symmetric-matrix representation and invariant binary form
proof_stages.summary: The speaker introduces V=Z^2⊗Sym^2 Z^n as pairs of symmetric n-by-n matrices (A,B), with invariant binary form F_v(x,y)=det(Ax-By), and reduces rational points on z^2=f to the existence of an integral pair whose invariant is f.

proof_stages.label: Orbit parametrization by orders and ideals
proof_stages.summary: For a binary form f, the Birch-Merriman/Nakagawa order R_f and modules I_f^k are constructed, and Wood's orbit classification identifies GL_n(Z)-orbits with fixed invariant f with equivalence classes of pairs (I,alpha) satisfying I^2⊂alpha I_f^{n-3} and a norm condition.

proof_stages.label: Construction of a lift from a square value
proof_stages.summary: Using the orbit parametrization, the talk shows that when an even-degree f represents a square, an explicit choice of alpha and ideal I produces an integral pair of symmetric matrices of a special sparse “star” shape with determinant f.

proof_stages.label: Geometry-of-numbers count of all lifts
proof_stages.summary: A fundamental domain for GL_n(Z) acting on V(R) is built from a bounded invariant-height slice and a Siegel-type domain, lattice points in the cusps are shown mostly to have reducible invariant form, and the remaining irreducible orbits have main term of order X^{n+1}, which by itself is not small enough.

proof_stages.label: Local pruning by star-shaped orbit conditions
proof_stages.summary: Because rational points yield only star-shaped lifts, the count is restricted to real and p-adic orbits that locally admit such a shape; comparing the few admissible local orbits with all local orbits replaces the main constant by one that decays exponentially in the genus.

proof_stages.label: Conclusion and comparison with marked-point families
proof_stages.summary: The local density estimates complete the proof of the stated no-rational-points result, after which the speaker contrasts the basepoint-free case with special hyperelliptic families having marked rational points that can be studied via Selmer groups and Chabauty.

examples.name: Odd-degree family with a marked Weierstrass point
examples.note: The family z^2=f with f of odd degree has a rational point at infinity, serving as a contrasting special family treated by Selmer-average and Chabauty methods rather than by the basepoint-free argument of the talk.
examples.engagement_reason: he treats the odd-degree case by running the orbit construction explicitly, taking alpha = 1 and I = I_f^{(n-3)/2} to produce a pair of symmetric matrices for f

passes.reason: the odd-degree model is a systematic pointed subfamily with a built-in rational Weierstrass point, discussed comparatively rather than as a refutation
The odd-degree hyperelliptic curves form a systematic special family with a built-in rational Weierstrass point, used for comparison.
The odd-degree model marks a rational Weierstrass point, i.e. a ramification/critical point of the hyperelliptic double cover, and is discussed as a special family rather than a refutation.
The odd-degree pointed hyperelliptic family systematically has a marked rational Weierstrass point and is discussed as a special comparison family, not to refute the density theorem.
The odd-degree hyperelliptic family is a special subfamily with a marked rational Weierstrass point, discussed for comparison rather than as a sporadic or adversarial example.
The odd-degree marked-Weierstrass family has a built-in rational point, obstructing any no-rational-points claim for that special family.
The odd-degree pointed family has a forced rational Weierstrass point, so the no-rational-points conclusion cannot apply to that subfamily.
The odd-degree hyperelliptic family with a marked rational Weierstrass point is a systematic special family discussed for comparison, not a disproof of the density claim.
the odd-degree family has a built-in rational Weierstrass point, obstructing any literal no-rational-points assertion for that subfamily.

examples.name: Low-genus proportions
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker notes that the methods already give about 20% Hasse-principle failures for genus-one quartics and more than 50% for genus-two hyperelliptic curves, illustrating the density statement in small genera.
examples.engagement_reason: he only states numerical low-genus proportions such as 20% in genus 1, over 50% in genus 2, and about 99% by genus 10 without deriving them in the talk

passes.reason: the g=1/2 figures are low-boundary genus cases cited as positive or majority proportions of Hasse-principle failures
Ordinary small-genus numerical density instances are used to illustrate the theorem rather than to refute a claim.
The cited genus-1 and genus-2 positive-density cases are ordinary low-genus hyperelliptic families whose counted members fail the Hasse principle.
The cited genus-1 and genus-2 proportions are lower-boundary genus cases, and the curves counted are locally soluble but rational-point-free Hasse-principle failures.
The genus 1 and genus 2 percentage calculations are used as ordinary illustrative low-genus instances of the density results, not to refute a claim.
The genus-one and genus-two proportions are lower-boundary cases of the genus parameter used to calibrate the theorem.
The genus-one/two proportions are lower-boundary checks of the genus parameter rather than failures of the theorem.

examples.name: Classical Hasse-principle counterexamples
examples.note: Lind-Reichardt binary quartics and Selmer's plane cubic are cited as historical instances of Hasse-principle failure, used to suggest that such failures are common rather than exceptional.
examples.engagement_reason: he cites the Linden-Reichardt and Selmer Hasse-principle counterexamples as classical examples and says such examples are common, but does no computation on them

passes.reason: the Lind-Reichardt and Selmer curves are ordinary genus-one-type curves used explicitly as Hasse-principle counterexamples

examples.name: Monic even-degree family with marked non-Weierstrass points
examples.note: The speaker contrasts the general even-degree case with monic even-degree models, where the two points at infinity are marked rational non-Weierstrass points and related counting/Selmer methods are expected to show that most curves have only those points.
examples.engagement_reason: he works through the even-degree square-representing case underlying marked non-Weierstrass points, constructing the ideal with a square root coefficient and writing explicit n=6 matrices whose determinant gives f

passes.reason: the monic even-degree model is a systematic pointed subfamily with the two rational non-Weierstrass points at infinity, discussed comparatively rather than adversarially
